item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is glorious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who was unfortunately a frequent visitor of websites like this circa 1998, I can say with certainty that the word "tsundere" didn't exist (on English websites, at least) back then.

The rest of it is startlingly spot-on. I experienced Vietnam-style flashbacks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i literally joined the site after being a long time lurker just so i could gush about this skip. this is genuinely something i have thought about every day since i read it the first time. the final image literally makes a scare chord play in my head every time i read the article, and im not even being hyperbolic.

im genuinely surprised how divisive the article is, i guess you had to be there (fandom in late 2000s/early 2010s), and also have modern knowledge of parasocial relationships and how fanfiction of real people affects those being written about. like, yes, starting with funny anime fanart of foundation personnel is a hard sell to someone who isnt "in the know"… but it was/is really like that. real life friendships have actually been destroyed with anime fanart and badly written fanfiction.

on a more personal level… i have a fear of surveillance, even when there is no reasonable way for any kind of recording device to be where i am, so this plays into that in a way that deeply terrifies me. the bit about 5403 itself ignoring f/f relationships to instead focus on f/m or m/m ones, even when theres a real/canon f/f relationship is something that not only rings true for fandom, but also for bi/lesbian women in real life, like myself.

tl;dr: haunts my every waking moment, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This… was an absolutely horrifying read. This really got to me in a way that's hard to describe. I, personally, do not agree with the common criticism that the article is too brief to effectively make the reader empathize with the characters; the emotions shown by STF Chi-11 came across very acutely to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who went through a cringy fujoshi phase, this hits way to hard at home. Those time where I read fanfics, ship male characters, not nothing about toxic fandoms.
shudders

Just take my +1 already.

You’ve just let me relive a part if my teenage years I would rather forget.
why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why. why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
disastrous.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks, i hate it

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i definitely liked this enough to +1 it. i've run into people like this in my high school and college days, and they are truly scarier than any neck snapping peanuts.

that being said, i definitely recommend taking the crit points presented here in the comments under consideration. there are definitely some weird tonal jerks in this one, and laraske could use more beef in her character writing. just food for thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could delete?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that this could be further refined, to give more color to Laraskë's character, and to add more evidence to the toll 5403 takes on its subjects; which in turn, would amplify the efficacy of the stinger.

The snippets of commentary provide a sobering foil for 5403's immaturity, but didn't give enough insight for me to see anyone as actual characters in their own right, but vehicles for story progression. While I adore Laraskë's tenacity and humility, I'm feel that I'm only given a glimpse of her as an individual too long after-the-fact; back-ended alongside the only substantial depictions of the sociological/psychological harm caused by 5403.

As it stands, I walked away from this without an acute feeling of the invasive, violating nature of 5403's obsessive observation and perverse intentions. Although this alluded to in the Conprocs and first addendum, 5403 seems to be mostly regarded as an annoyance which grows into mild frustration. Thus, the final image comes with all the terror of a pop-up ad. It feels dismissable.
Nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!!! That's pretty much my exact intention, I'm really glad it got through with you =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus fucking Christ this is horrible. Not scary-horrible, just deeply weird and uncomfortable, the kind of parody that goes just a *bit* too far to be entirely funny and flirts with being jarringly tragic. An easy +1 from me. Well done.

ETA: I think the best way to sum this up is that it's the sort of parody that presents a seriously horrific and realistic situation — an obsessive stalker who is literally watching you sleep — in a deliberately cutesy way, and in my opinion, the tonal mismatch elevates the horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: when the scp mentioned how it wanted to push them into the vorehole i lost it. +1 this is amazing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edit: nevermind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, this doesn't make me uncomfortable or weirded out; it moreso felt frustrating. It's a vapid critique of parasocial relationships cloaked in humor that just relies on "haha remember fanfic?". And, as sirpudding and Woedenaz mentioned upthread, the Foundation feels bizarre and out-of-character1 throughout the first half. It's a concept that could go a lot further, but it's hindered by not knowing what it's doing and shooting itself in the foot with dark comedy.

Footnotes
1. Ironic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bankruptcy? Or something else?

No, Yahoo just decided that the free webhost business wasn't profitable any more. Because this is Geocities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't diggin' it at first, but I kept reading and I wasn't disappointed! This is an excellent scip!

This was hard to read; but it wasn't bad (the skip itself wasn't bad; the messages of the anomaly were… spiritually taxing.)

This definitely reminds me of the general creepiness that comes with seeing the shipping of real life people, and how some internet folk get weirdly obsessed over it.

This made me uncomfortable, but T'm pretty sure that was your intention so +1 for a smashing success

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a feeling that this is one of those things that only resonates deeply if you were in that culture or were deeply familiar with it. Also, thanks for your help in making it as awful as possible I guess ;__;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly surprised at the mixed reception. As someone who's been around "fandom" since middle school, this is a rather frightening take on the RPF phenomenon, and especially how the utter disregard for canon we take for granted in fiction doesn't exactly mesh well when real people are involved.

Also the, uh, ending. That's… that's a thing.

item.title: That ending
item.content:encoded:
Wow

Review
Itsa review

Reading through the logged posts at first was extremely entertaining, and I have to admit that the inclusion of illustrations caught me offguard and helped a lot with my enjoyment of them.

I will say that in the latter half of the article things took a hard turn down into the dark and serious, it doesnt totally work initally? At least for me, its a little bit too jarring to go from the more lighthearted writing from the stuff written by the skip to the serious dialogue in the interview (though this is also probably a positive since I was so into the "posts" as I was reading) but is still effective nonetheless, esspecially with the "response" post by the skip to the Doc.

By far the most effective part (to me at least) was the ending. I'm not sure if you actually created an article on ao3 to do the screencap, but jeez is it effective.

Overall, a really nicely done skip! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm… this is a tough one. I've been glancing at this since the drafting stages on Discord and IRC since it was popping up now and then. From what I've seen, this is really great writing and a solid tone on clinical language, especially for a first SCP, so kudos to you.

+ general stuff
- hide

However, like some had addressed earlier on, unfortunately, the whole moral dilemma angle didn't feel… fleshed out enough? The first half was mostly about how this SCP operates, but when it came to the latter half where we begin to delve into the sheer amount of trauma this is dealing on Chi-11 (especially Dr. Laraskë and Researcher Harrison), it felt… weirdly underdeveloped for me to really sympathize with them for the ending to hit hard? Hm. The thing where 5403 begins to notice Dr. Laraskë as a huge obstacle to its plans came a bit too late for me to get a feel of her suffering cuz of its extensive degree of a breach of privacy? Like, this could be a fantastic opportunity to delve more into that aspect about someone/something watching us even in our own bedroom considering modern times, where the SCP parallels some governments that plan to keep watch on its citizens, but that aspect wasn't really… touched on a little more?

Hm. It's very clear that a lot of effort went into this, and I really dig the pictures and the "fake post" style by 5403, but I think the primary conflict regarding the stress Chi-11 is getting from the breach of privacy felt a little not fleshed out. Novote.

+ some very nitpick stuff
- hide

This:

SCP-5403 has displayed the capacity to access comprehensive audiovisual surveillance of the Foundation through unknown means. The content of the site exclusively relates to observations derived from this surveillance.

and

The majority (approximately eighty-five percent) consist of commentary on current events relating to Foundation interests.

Kinda sound similar, and hence just a little repetitive? Maybe you could try to move "SCP-5403 is similar to a weblog in structure…" paragraph up after the first paragraph of the description and then try to combine the two aforementioned paragraphs together? It could help a little better in terms of flowing from talking about the SCP being a website in general to the specifics of what its posts contain.

I was initially a little confused about the first post, because I wasn't sure if that was meant to be the discovery or like, just another one of 5403's fanfic posts during containment. Maybe make it a little clearer beforehand that this was how 5403 was discovered? Idk.

SPC-████ is obviously just a [DATA EXPUNGED]

Is the SPC thing like, an intentional reference to the GoI, a misspelling by 5403 or is it meant to be "SCP"?

G███████, the hosting service for SCP-5403, was shut down permanently by its parent company.

Bankruptcy? Or something else? I was wondering if like, the Foundation could've fabricated some evidence of some shady stuff going on behind the company and filed a report against them as a means of pressuring them to shut down 5403's host server earlier on before Chi-11 had to suffer? Idk, I think this is how I headcanon them in this case; you're free to take this with a grain of salt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much, i really appreciate it :']

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy, this is cursed….

Anyway, I do like this idea: obsessive fangirl for the Foundation, who loves to pair the males together…. but for some strange reason, does not seem to notice any of the same-sex female relationships.

I also like how it eventually goes into disturbing territory: first, the SCP "whitewashing" Laraske of her lesbian relationship, then leaking info that the personnel really do not want to read, till the grand denounement when the SCP goes full hatefic writer (and I know a real horrid one)

That said….

I do not feel there was much for the readers to really connect with the affected characters. At least for me. Not even the notes and the interview logs were sufficient (though they do stand to show how such a seemingly comedic figure is actually rather distressing to the people who actually have to deal with it, how a comedic trope is something you would not like in real life)

Nonetheless….
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using my first comment on a scip to say I really liked this! I mean, I'm a simple man, so honestly just the stupid otaku fangirlese alone had already gotten me feeling pretty positive, it's something I'm weak to, but I personally didn't find the Laraskë development rushed, and the final click was a huge sucker punch. The fact that venting is being allowed in the official documentation already feels like a big enough concession to narrative over form, I don't think it needs to linger even further over the head researcher's headspace rather than the thing itself.

Nit-pick: Assuming 5403's meant to be writing culturally from the position of 1998, the use of "waifu" is anachronistic, since that's a late aughts thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no-voting this but I've been mulling it over since reading.

There's a number of aspects that are bothering me, some of which have already been mentioned.

First off, the rather laissez faire attitude of The Foundation throughout irked me a fair bit. They redirect the IP of the site1 through some means but there appears to be little attempt in figuring out how the site is getting this information. The only real mention of it is to say that it is "unknown." This immediately begs the question of what they have tried, if they've discovered anything about it's methods at all? We come in to this with all of the characters seemingly resigned to this site being sort of an inevitability but not given any context to why they feel that way.

So much of this was The Foundation just watching and being reactive rather than being proactive, which just seems a bit odd.

Besides that, I think the overall article just felt a little confused in what it was trying to do and feels a fair bit like lost potential on a number of aspects. I could definitely see this concept coming off as very creepy and unsettling but the vast majority of the creepy aspects get played off for laughs, causing it to lose all tension. The hardest hitting part is definitely the final image but it doesn't feel earned.

There's certainly a great, creepy story to tell here about parasocial relationships and obsessively identifying with fictional characters but I don't believe this quite reaches it.

Footnotes
1. It's kind of minor but I also want to mention that, if this site was meant to be on Geocities, I am fairly positive this sort of thing wouldn't work. Geocities sites all had the same hosting IP address since they were all hosted by Geocities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tries to be a critique or analysis on parasocial relationships but is wildly disparate — intentionally or not, it plays the concept for dark humor in the first section and then backloads all of the conflict into the latter half, and not even particularly well — most of the conflict is just laid out in the interview, it's just being explained to the reader. The stinger is nice but it doesn't make up for the fact that it doesn't really even know what it's being a stinger to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stuff like this is horrifying in it's own right… But having your personal info being outed in this sort of format is extremely unsettling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The initial assumption should have been moles and bugs. If you hear hoofbeats, think horses not zebras. It still could be conventional espionage, just by undetected means. This should have at least started as a counterintelligence operation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anomalous aspect is the fact that it has surveillance capabilities without any apparent camera or infrastructure to support this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is mostly just a security breach and the anomalous aspects seem tenuous and wouldn't have been apparent initially besides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like it aims to be an interesting mix between social commentary/character piece but doesn't have the necessary writing quality to back this whole concept up.

The whole fanfiction theme was laughed at on SCP and other similar online media for quite some time, and this one doesn't really provide something new in its representation. The parody that comes from the excerpts of 5403's behaviour is over-the-top to the point where it immediately becomes what their purpose is: to tell us that 5403 is bad. The problem is that they're trying way too hard and end up lacking any kind of subtlety. I have similar problems with crosslinks of some old lolfoundation stuff as well, generally you don't have to point at things you're parodying for the parody to work. Such approach can work in some cases, but here it mostly nullifies the satirical implications the piece could've had.

I don't find the character part of the piece that moving, too. I sorta like the ending, but the lack of the researcher's reaction and further actions that would provide any kind of context is missing, and that last message ends up feeling like it's ok and should be that way. While I generally feel like the concept can be made into something interesting (trying to ruin someone's life because they're ruining your special little fantasy world? That's hard-hitting because it happens IRL), the current execution is too weak to pull this off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna come forward and admit that I was dishonest when reviewing this. I didn't wanna come across as mean, so I ignored the aspects I didn't like, which is most of it, and focused on the character development and so bad they're good lines. My honest feedback is that the anomaly is not painted as the villain for doing this, but is played up for laughs, and that makes it harder to read.

I apologize immensely for not being honest. It will not happen again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't enjoy it. I'm usually open minded and I even accept more playful narratives with twists like this, but the fanfic stuff1 is something I can't fathom to read, less here.

The rest of the article doesn't do anything to recover from that -1

Footnotes
1. The deliberately bad written bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like downvoting things so I'm going to leave this as a novote, but I didn't enjoy the piece very much. It felt like it relied too heavily on humor that just didn't land for me. The dialogue was okay, I didn't find much fault with it, but overall I just think the writing was… mediocre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This brings back some very horrid memories during my time on the internet, and I mean that in the best possible way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An opening of "anime UwU Bright" is going to need to work very hard to sell me in a non-joke context, and trying to play that for horror in-and-of-itself doesn't succeed for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrific in more ways than one, mainly because of how close it comes to simulating actual internet culture. +1 with gusto, this is good shit.

Edit: Apologies for the second comment in case I annoy any mods. My first post didn't show before, so I thought it didn't go through. It only appeared to me after posting this comment. I'm sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is nothing about this that I enjoyed. It plays on some of the worst aspects of the early internet that I would just rather forget.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think my main problems with this piece ultimately come from the framing.

As I said in IRC, it's quite obvious what you're attempting to hit for; for the reader to recognize how strange and disturbing 5403's actions are. The SCP ultimately falls flat in a couple of areas in that regard, for me at least.

  • The length and lack of characterization of Laraske besides "oh no i hate what the scp is doing and i'm tired of it." It reduces the emotional connection to, in essence, a bit piece that you attempt to hammer home with the final collapsible, and due to this, I feel really nothing towards it because of this one-dimensionalness of Laraske.
  • The main conflict doesn't feel like a convincing portrayal. In my opinion, yes it is written well; the issue is that once the sort of "funny shock value" of the art and language is over, it becomes a bit of a slog to get through. It doesn't feel as if it's getting particularly worse up till the very end, where it goes from 0 to 100 really quickly and leaves no room for breathing. That motive and idea you're aiming for is hidden beneath the exact same framing and word choice you use to do humor just above, and it makes it difficult to see your message throughout the entire piece.
  • As barred said, the actual emotional portrayal of Laraske is iffy; stays too long, or needs to be fleshed out.
  • I don't have a problem with the topic you're attempting to cover, but merely the method of how you go about it; as stormbreath said, it's very, very hard to have an article pull off an impactful story when it says "anime uwu bright" in the very beginnings, and this article never recovered from that dissonance until the end, where it did so quickly, and unconvincingly.

There's certainly potential in this, but the strange pacing and lack of compelling characters reduced a piece attempting to tug at the heartstrings to an unfunny slog.

Minor edit: spelling and clarifications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is simultaneously one of the most horrific and most glorious things I have ever seen on the site. +1 with gusto

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… ambivalent on this one. To some degree, I do find the premise of Laraske being distressed by all this personal stuff coming from 5403 an interesting one. Unfortunately, when it comes to actually conveying that distress, it either doesn't stay long enough for me to feel enough sympathy (see: Laraske's notes in the first addendum being sidetracked by something about the redacted SCP or more unrelated fanfiction shenanigans) or overstays its welcome (see: the interview with Laraske in the second addendum).

This is a hard balance to strike, I'll admit: indeed, finding a balance between sidestepping Laraske's distress and overly delving into it while also keeping in mind the format of an SCP article is definitely a tough one. However, there are some main pieces of advice which I would say would make the piece more enjoyable. Particularly, having a better lead-in to Laraske being distressed by 5403 (as Clef x Bright shipping, while somewhat funny, doesn't do the best job at leading into Laraske's storyline) or giving Laraske's emotions some time to breathe instead of fragmenting them into researcher's notes would give me some room to empathize with this character a lot more.

Because of all the above, I've downvoted, but there is a lot of potential with an idea like this. I just think that its emotional centerpiece could have been executed a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The scariest part about this is that it reminds me of me in 2010. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: uwu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take my "what the fuck" and get outta here. +1, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvotes

"Pssh… nothin personnel… kid."

item.title: authorpost
item.content:encoded:
Hi my name is Ebony Dark’ness Dementia Raven Way
and I have long ebony black hair (that’s how I got my name)
with purple streaks and red tips that reaches my mid-back
and icy blue eyes like limpid tears and a lot of people tell me I look like Amy Lee
(AN: if u don’t know who she is get da hell out of here!).

Thanks to The Pighead, Tyumen, TheHouseOfBalloons, Calibri Bold, Greyve, UraniumEmpire, and AbsentmindedNihilist for critting this trainwreck, Orchitty and DianaBerry for helping brainstorm and supporting me through the conceptual stages, and to Raddagher for the email template in the last collapsible.

Special thanks to daviiid for performing the immense sacrifice of drawing me shitty anime men and letting me put glitter on their faces.

the number 5403 is a reference to the HTTP Forbidden error. let the record show that i am very sorry about putting catboy glitterbright on the wiki. i'm counting this as my first SCP because it's the first one i put actual effort into

edit: i knew this was going to be polarizing but WOW

Image cred:

+ show block
– hide block

First and third images drawn by daviiid, released under CC-BY-SA 3.0, and edited by me. Second image drawn by me and released under CC-BY-SA 3.0.
